From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Directional divisions marked on a compass

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Compass-rose-32-pt.svg/500px-Compass-rose-32-pt.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Compass-rose-32-pt.svg)

32-point [compass rose](/wiki/Compass_rose "Compass rose")

The **points of the compass** are a set of horizontal, [radially](/wiki/Radius "Radius") arrayed compass directions (or [azimuths](/wiki/Azimuth#In_navigation "Azimuth")) used in [navigation](/wiki/Navigation "Navigation") and [cartography](/wiki/Cartography "Cartography"). A *[compass rose](/wiki/Compass_rose "Compass rose")* is primarily composed of four [cardinal directions](/wiki/Cardinal_direction "Cardinal direction")вАФ[north](/wiki/North "North"), [east](/wiki/East "East"), [south](/wiki/South "South"), and [west](/wiki/West "West")вАФeach separated by 90 [degrees](/wiki/Degree_(angle) "Degree (angle)"), and secondarily divided by four ordinal (intercardinal) directionsвАФnortheast, southeast, southwest, and northwestвАФeach located halfway between two cardinal directions. Some disciplines such as [meteorology](/wiki/Meteorology "Meteorology") and navigation further divide the compass with additional azimuths. Within European tradition, a fully defined compass has 32 "points" (and any finer subdivisions are described in fractions of points).[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Compass points or compass directions are valuable in that they allow a user to refer to a specific azimuth in a [colloquial](/wiki/Colloquialism "Colloquialism") fashion, without having to compute or remember degrees.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Designations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Points_of_the_compass&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Designations")]

Several terms redirect here. For other uses, see [Northeast (disambiguation)](/wiki/Northeast_(disambiguation) "Northeast (disambiguation)"), [Northwest (disambiguation)](/wiki/Northwest_(disambiguation) "Northwest (disambiguation)"), [Southeast (disambiguation)](/wiki/Southeast_(disambiguation) "Southeast (disambiguation)"), and [Southwest (disambiguation)](/wiki/Southwest_(disambiguation) "Southwest (disambiguation)").

The names of the compass point directions follow these rules:

### 8-wind compass rose

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Points_of_the_compass&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 8-wind compass rose")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Compass_rose_en_08p.svg/250px-Compass_rose_en_08p.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Compass_rose_en_08p.svg)

8-wind compass rose

* The four [cardinal directions](/wiki/Cardinal_direction "Cardinal direction") are north (N), east (E), south (S), west (W), at 90¬∞ angles on the compass rose.
* The four intercardinal (or ordinal) directions are formed by bisecting the above, giving: northeast (NE), southeast (SE), southwest (SW), and northwest (NW). In English and many other tongues, these are [compound words](/wiki/Compound_(linguistics) "Compound (linguistics)"). Different [style guides](/wiki/Style_guide "Style guide") for the four mandate spaces, dashes, or none.
  + In Bulgarian, Catalan, Czech, Danish, Dutch, English, Esperanto, French, Galician, German, Greek, Hungarian, Ido, Italian, Japanese (usually), Macedonian, Norwegian (both Bokmal and Nynorsk), Polish, Portuguese, Romanian, Romansch, Russian, Serbian, Croatian, Spanish, Swedish, Ukrainian, and Welsh the part meaning north or south precedes the part meaning east or west.[[3]](#cite_note-wikt-3)
  + In Chinese, Vietnamese, Gaelic, and less commonly Japanese, the part meaning east or west precedes the other.
  + In Estonian, Finnish, Breton, the "Italianate system" (see section "Traditional Mediterranean compass points" below), and many South Asian and Southeast Asian languages such as Telugu, the intercardinals have distinct words.[[3]](#cite_note-wikt-3)
* The eight *principal winds* (or *main winds*) are the [set union](/wiki/Set_union "Set union") of the cardinals and intercardinals. Taken in turn, each is 45¬∞ from the next. These form the *8-wind compass rose*, the rose at its usual basic level today.

### 16-wind compass rose

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Points_of_the_compass&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 16-wind compass rose")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Brosen_windrose.svg/250px-Brosen_windrose.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Brosen_windrose.svg)

16-point [compass rose](/wiki/Compass_rose "Compass rose")

* The eight *half-winds* are the direction points obtained by bisecting the angles between the principal winds. The half-winds are north-northeast (NNE), east-northeast (ENE), east-southeast (ESE), south-southeast (SSE), south-southwest (SSW), west-southwest (WSW), west-northwest (WNW), and north-northwest (NNW). The name of each half-wind is constructed by combining the names of the principal winds to either side, with the cardinal wind coming first and the intercardinal wind second.
* The eight principal winds and the eight half-winds together form the *16-wind compass rose*, with each compass point at a 22+1вБД2¬∞ angle from its two neighbours.

* The sixteen *quarter-winds* are the direction points obtained by bisecting the angles between the points on the 16-wind compass rose (above). The quarter-winds are as follows:[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)
  + in NE quadrant: north by east (NbE), northeast by north (NEbN), northeast by east (NEbE), and east by north (EbN);
  + in SE quadrant: east by south (EbS), southeast by east (SEbE), southeast by south (SEbS), and south by east (SbE);
  + in SW quadrant: south by west (SbW), southwest by south (SWbS), southwest by west (SWbW), and west by south (WbS);
  + in NW quadrant: west by north (WbN), northwest by west (NWbW), northwest by north (NWbN), and north by west (NbW)
* All of the points in the 16-wind compass rose plus the sixteen quarter-winds together form the *32-wind compass rose*.
* If breaking down for study/signalling the subcomponents are called the "principal" followed by the "cardinal" wind/direction. As a [mnemonic](/wiki/Mnemonic "Mnemonic") (memory device), minds familiar encode the meaning of "X by Y" as "one small measure *from* X *towards* Y". It can be noted such measure ('one point') is 11+1вБД4¬∞. So, for example, "northeast by east" means "one quarter of the gap from NE towards E".

In summary, the 32-wind compass rose comes from the eight principal winds, eight half-winds, and sixteen quarter-winds combined, with each compass point at an 11+1вБД4¬∞ angle from the next.

#### Half- and quarter-points

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Points_of_the_compass&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Half- and quarter-points")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Bowditch1916p15CompassRose.png/250px-Bowditch1916p15CompassRose.png)](/wiki/File:Bowditch1916p15CompassRose.png)

Compass rose from *American Practical Navigator*, 1916

By the middle of the 18th century, the 32-point system had been further extended by using half- and quarter-points to give a total of 128 directions.[[6]](#cite_note-Chambers1743-6)
These fractional points are named by appending, for example, вБ†1/4вБ†east, вБ†1/2вБ†east, or вБ†3/4вБ†east to the name of one of the 32 points. Each of the 96 fractional points can be named in two ways, depending on which of the two adjoining whole points is used, for example, NвБ†3/4вБ†E is equivalent to NbEвБ†1/4вБ†N. Either form is easily understood, but alternative conventions as to correct usage developed in different countries and organisations. "It is the custom in the United States Navy to box *from* north and south *toward* east and west, with the exception that divisions adjacent to a cardinal or inter-cardinal point are always referred to that point."[[7]](#cite_note-7) The Royal Navy used the additional "rule that quarter points were never read from a point beginning and ending with the same letter".[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Compass roses very rarely named the fractional points and only showed small, unlabelled markers as a guide for helmsmen.

#### Maritime Use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Points_of_the_compass&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Maritime Use")]

Prior to the modern [three-figure method](/w/index.php?title=Three-figure_method&action=edit&redlink=1 "Three-figure method (page does not exist)") of describing directions (using the 360¬∞ of a circle), the 32-point compass was used for directions on most ships, especially among European crews. The smallest unit of measure recognized was 'one point', 1/32 of a circle, or 11+1вБД4¬∞.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9) In the mariner's exercise of "boxing the compass", all thirty-two points of the [compass](/wiki/Compass "Compass") are named in [clockwise](/wiki/Clockwise "Clockwise") order.[[10]](#cite_note-10) This exercise became more significant as navigation improved and the half- and quarter-point system increased the number of directions to include in the 'boxing'. Points remained the standard unit until switching to the three-figure degree method. These points were also used for relative measurement, so that an obstacle might be noted as 'two points off the starboard bow', meaning two points clockwise of straight ahead, 22+1вБД2¬∞[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9) This relative measurement may still be used in shorthand on modern ships, especially for handoffs between outgoing and incoming helmsmen, as the loss of granularity is less significant than the brevity and simplicity of the summary.

### 128 compass directions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Points_of_the_compass&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 128 compass directions")]

The table below shows how each of the 128 directions are named. The first two columns give the number of points and degrees clockwise from north. The third gives the equivalent bearing to the nearest degree from north or south towards east or west. The "CW" column gives the fractional-point bearings increasing in the [clockwise](/wiki/Clockwise "Clockwise") direction and "CCW" [counterclockwise](/wiki/Counterclockwise "Counterclockwise"). The final three columns show three common naming conventions: No "by" avoids the use of "by" with fractional points, U.S. Navy increases counterclockwise from a cardinal directly to the previous direction and clockwise elsewhere, and Royal Navy is the same but also uses counterclockwise from S to SbE to SSE and N to NbW to NNW. Colour coding shows whether each of the three naming systems matches the "CW" or "CCW" column.

## Traditional Mediterranean compass points

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Points_of_the_compass&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Traditional Mediterranean compass points")]

The traditional compass rose of eight winds (and its 16-wind and 32-wind derivatives) was invented by seafarers in the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") during the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") (with no obvious connection to the twelve [classical compass winds](/wiki/Classical_compass_winds "Classical compass winds") of the ancient Greeks and Romans). The traditional mariner's wind names were expressed in [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), or more precisely, the Italianate [Mediterranean lingua franca](/wiki/Mediterranean_lingua_franca "Mediterranean lingua franca") common among sailors in the 13th and 14th centuries, which was principally composed of Genoese ([Ligurian](/wiki/Ligurian_language_(Romance) "Ligurian language (Romance)")), mixed with [Venetian](/wiki/Venetian_language "Venetian language"), [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian_language "Sicilian language"), [Proven√Іal](/wiki/Proven%C3%A7al_dialect "Proven√Іal dialect"), [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language"), [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"), and [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") terms from around the Mediterranean basin.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/32-point_compass_%28traditional_winds%29.svg/500px-32-point_compass_%28traditional_winds%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:32-point_compass_(traditional_winds).svg)

32-wind compass with traditional names (and traditional colour code)

This Italianate [patois](/wiki/Patois "Patois") was used to designate the names of the principal winds on the compass rose found in mariners' compasses and [portolan charts](/wiki/Portolan_chart "Portolan chart") of the 14th and 15th centuries. The traditional names of the eight principal winds are:

* (N) вАУ *Tramontana*
* (NE) вАУ *Greco* (or *[Bora](/wiki/Bora_(wind) "Bora (wind)")* in some Venetian sources)
* (E) вАУ *Levante* (sometimes *Oriente*)
* (SE) вАУ *Scirocco* (or *Exaloc* in Catalan)
* (S) вАУ *Ostro* (or *Mezzogiorno* in Venetian)
* (SW) вАУ *Libeccio* (or *Garbino*, *Eissalot* in Proven√Іal)
* (W) вАУ *Ponente* (or *Zephyrus* in Greek)
* (NW) вАУ *Maestro* (or *Mistral* in Proven√Іal)

Local spelling variations are far more numerous than listed, e.g. Tramutana. Traditional compass roses will typically have the initials T, G, L, S, O, L, P, and M on the main points. Portolan charts also colour-coded the compass winds: black for the eight principal winds, green for the eight half-winds, and red for the sixteen quarter-winds.

Each half-wind name is simply a combination of the two principal winds that it bisects, with the shortest name usually placed first, for example: NNE is "Greco-Tramontana"; ENE is "Greco-Levante"; SSE is "Ostro-Scirocco", etc. The quarter winds are expressed with an Italian phrase, "*Quarto di* X *verso* Y" (pronounced [[ЋИkwarto di *X* ЋИv…Ыrso *Y*]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian")[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) one quarter from X towards Y), or "X *al* Y" (X to Y) or "X *per* Y" (X by Y). There are no irregularities to trip over; the closest principal wind always comes first, the more distant one second, for example: north-by-east is "*Quarto di Tramontana verso Greco*"; and northeast-by-north is "*Quarto di Greco verso Tramontana*".

The table below shows how the 32 compass points are named. Each point has an angular range of 11+1вБД4 degrees where the [azimuth](/wiki/Azimuth "Azimuth") *midpoint* is the horizontal angular direction (clockwise from north) of the given compass bearing; *minimum* is the lower (counterclockwise) angular limit of the compass point; and *maximum* is the upper (clockwise) angular limit of the compass point.

## Chinese compass points

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Points_of_the_compass&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Chinese compass points")]

Navigation texts dating from the [Yuan](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty"), [Ming](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty"), and [Qing](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") dynasties in China use a 24-pointed compass with named directions. These are based on the twelve [Earthly Branches](/wiki/Earthly_Branches "Earthly Branches"), which also form the basis of the Chinese zodiac. When a single direction is specified, it may be prefaced by the character еЦЃ (meaning single) or дЄє.
